description: The provided image displays a 'Check for updates' user-interface element resembling a publisher CrossMark badge, featuring a circular icon with a red bookmark symbol enclosed by a yellow-golden ring, with the text 'Check for updates' positioned directly beneath it. This visual appears to be a platform or publisher interface component rather than a scientific figure containing experimental data, micrographs, spectra, or plots pertinent to the Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel study or its warm multiaxial forging behavior. No material systems, sample identifiers, processing conditions, phase constituents, microstructural features, mechanical curves, or performance metrics are visually present. The figure caption and extracted text are empty, with no citation sentences available to associate this image with the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive modeling discussed in the manuscript title. Consequently, the image lacks identifiable subfigures, data trends, spectral peaks, or morphological details that would support core scientific conclusions regarding plastic flow behavior.

Figure 1 presents a schematic flow diagram illustrating the multiaxial forging (MAF) process applied to Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel. The diagram traces the complete deformation sequence starting from an original rectangular sample with dimensions 20 × 20 × 30 through three successive compression steps (1st, 2nd, and 3rd compression), interspersed with 90° rotations about the y, z, and x axes to cyclically alter the pressing direction. Color-coded cubic blocks (blue, green, and orange faces) and an embedded x-y-z coordinate system allow visual tracking of crystallographic/geometric orientation changes after each rotation and compression. An inset coordinate frame defines the three fundamental directions during forging: pressing, constrained (die-bound), and flow directions. The sequence demonstrates that the sample is compressed along one axis, expands in the flow direction, and remains geometrically constrained in the third direction, ultimately yielding a deformed sample with restored 20 × 20 × 30 dimensions but accumulated multiaxial plastic strain. This figure establishes the experimental boundary conditions and rotation protocol central to studying plastic flow behavior under warm multiaxial forging.

The figure presents a macroscopic photograph of forged billets and machined dog-bone tensile specimens of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed by warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The top row shows three forged billets marked with identifiers corresponding to MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5, arranged from left to right. The bottom row displays matching pairs of tensile samples cut from each respective billet. A centimeter-scale ruler positioned below the specimens provides macroscopic dimensional reference, indicating billet lengths on the order of 20–25 mm. Visual inspection reveals progressive surface roughening, variations in surface oxide or discoloration, and subtle shape changes as the number of forging passes increases. The caption and accompanying text state that these specimens represent sequential stages of severe plastic deformation, and that the material could only withstand five total passes before fracturing on the sixth attempt. This figure documents the physical evolution and integrity of the forged material, validating the experimental conditions for subsequent tensile testing and plastic flow analysis using a modified Johnson-Cook model.

Figure 3 presents schematic illustrations defining the specimen geometry and sampling strategy for warm multiaxial forging (MAF) of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel. Panel (a) depicts a dimensionally annotated cuboid MAF sample (including a 30 mm base width and segmented height or depth markers of 20 mm and 10 mm) with orthogonal directional arrows indicating the flow, constrained, and pressing directions. It highlights a blue-shaded “Analysed area for XRD, EBSD” targeted for microstructural characterization, while circular icons on the right face denote locations where miniature tensile specimens were extracted after MAF-0, MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5 passes, corresponding to cumulative equivalent strains of 0, 0.47, 1.4, and 2.34 as reported in the text. Panel (b) details the miniature round tensile specimen geometry with all dimensions in millimeters: gauge length 16 mm, total length 26.5 mm, gauge diameter Ø4 mm, grip diameter Ø7.2 mm, and transition fillet radius R1. Collectively, these schematics establish the standardized spatial reference framework enabling direct correlation between evolving microstructure (grain size, dislocation density) and tensile properties across increasing MAF strains.

The figure presents a half-sectional view of a 3-D finite-element model simulating closed-die forging of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel before and after compression. The left subfigure shows the initial undeformed workpiece (blue mesh, 30 mm × 20 mm × 20 mm) enclosed by rigid dies (grey), while the right subfigure shows the deformed geometry after a 10 mm punch displacement along the Y-direction. As stated in the accompanying text, the workpiece was meshed with 41,382 C3D8R elements (0.6 mm size) and modeled as a deformable body against rigid tooling in ABAQUS/Explicit, using a friction coefficient of 0.15. Visual comparison reveals that the billet compresses in the Y-direction and bulges in the X-direction (flow direction), whereas deformation in the Z-direction is suppressed by the die wall, consistent with the reported multiaxial forging boundary conditions. This figure demonstrates that the explicit dynamic FE model successfully captures the macroscopic deformation fields and validates the simulation setup for mapping pass-to-pass deformation history in warm multiaxial forging.

Figure 5 presents X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF) for 0, 1, 3, and 5 passes. The bottom trace (MAF-0) exhibits sharp γ-austenite peaks indexed as γ(111), γ(200), γ(220), γ(311), and γ(222), along with a κ(220) reflection from κ-carbides. MAF-1 shows additional δ(211) and κ(222) peaks with reduced overall intensity. With increasing MAF passes (MAF-1 to MAF-5), the diffraction peaks progressively broaden and diminish in intensity, indicating increased dislocation density and grain refinement. The patterns confirm, as stated in the caption and text, that carbide phases remain essentially unchanged during MAF, allowing precipitation strengthening to be treated as constant. The peak broadening data were used to quantify dislocation density, which together with EBSD-measured grain sizes, was incorporated into a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model to predict strain-dependent yield strength evolution.

This figure presents the evolution of dislocation density as a function of equivalent plastic strain for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging. The chart plots measured dislocation density values (black squares) against a predicted saturation curve (red dashed line) generated using Eq. (7). The vertical axis denotes dislocation density in m^-2, while the horizontal axis represents equivalent plastic strain up to 2.5. Experimental data reveal a rapid initial increase in dislocation density that gradually saturates at higher strain levels. The predicted curve follows the exponential relation rho = 7.96E14 + k1*(1-exp(-k2*epsilon)), with fitted constants k1 = 7.90516E15 +/- 5.28171E14 m^-2 and k2 = 1.81319 +/- 0.42469, yielding an R-square of 0.98554. The caption states that the initial density rho_0 corresponds to the unstrained MAF-0 condition. This figure supports the core conclusion that dislocation multiplication and saturation during warm deformation are accurately captured by the exponential model, furnishing essential parameters for the modified Johnson-Cook constitutive description.

This figure presents EBSD image quality (IQ) maps (a–d) and inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (e–h) along the normal/pressing direction for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF). Subfigures (a)–(d) correspond to MAF-0 (solutionized and quenched), MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5, respectively, with green and red lines denoting low-angle grain boundaries (LAGBs, 1°–15°) and high-angle grain boundaries (HAGBs, 15°–180°). The HAGB fraction decreases from 0.622 in MAF-0 to 0.240 in MAF-3 while the LAGB fraction peaks at 0.759, indicating substructure formation; after 5 passes, HAGBs increase sharply to 0.699 due to dynamic recrystallization. The IPF maps (e–h) reveal that MAF-0 exhibits a strong texture with significant grains having [101] and [111] directions parallel to the pressing direction, whereas increasing MAF passes progressively randomize the crystallographic orientation. These observations confirm that warm MAF initially promotes geometrically necessary boundaries and ultimately leads to grain refinement and texture randomization.

This figure presents two related plots on grain structure evolution in Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during multiaxial forging (MAF). Subfigure (a) displays area fraction versus grain size distributions for MAF-0 (as-solutionized), MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5 samples. With increasing deformation passes, the distribution shifts toward smaller grain sizes and becomes narrower, indicating progressive grain refinement and homogenization. Subfigure (b) plots measured grain size against equivalent plastic strain alongside an exponential fit predicted using Eq. (8) (d = 50·exp(−k₃·ε), k₃ ≈ 0.64, R² ≈ 0.99). The measured grain size decreases from an initial ~50 μm to ~35 μm, ~20 μm, and ~13 μm after one, three, and five passes (total strain ≈ 2.3), respectively. Together, the plots demonstrate that MAF induces substantial grain refinement through multidirectional compression, providing microstructural data for the strain-dependent constitutive model.

This figure presents Kernel Average Misorientation (KAM) maps derived from EBSD analysis of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel processed by warm multiaxial forging (MAF). Panels (a) through (d) correspond to samples subjected to 0, 1, 3, and 5 MAF passes (MAF-0, MAF-1, MAF-3, and MAF-5), respectively, with a scale bar of 60 μm and a color scale from 0° (blue) to 5° (red). The average KAM values monotonically increase from 0.34° for MAF-0 to 0.72° for MAF-1, 0.87° for MAF-3, and 1.07° for MAF-5, reflecting progressive accumulation of geometrically necessary dislocations and increased local lattice curvature with cumulative strain. The microstructure transitions from homogeneous blue-dominated regions indicating low misorientation to increasingly green and red networks signifying high dislocation densities and substructure formation. These experimental observations corroborate the numerical simulation findings on dislocation evolution and the inverse relationship between dislocation density and grain size during warm MAF.

Figure 10 presents engineering stress–strain curves of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8 C low-density steel after warm multiaxial forging (MAF) for 0, 1, 3, and 5 passes, labeled MAF-0 through MAF-5. The solutionized coarse-grained MAF-0 specimen exhibits the lowest yield strength (~380 MPa) and the largest uniform elongation, reaching an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~762 MPa with pronounced work hardening. With increasing MAF passes, both yield and tensile strengths rise markedly while ductility drops sharply: MAF-1 reaches ~1040 MPa yield and ~1368 MPa UTS; MAF-3 reaches ~1279 MPa yield and ~1415 MPa UTS; and MAF-5 achieves the highest yield of ~1528 MPa and UTS of ~1548 MPa but with minimal work hardening (~20 MPa) and the shortest elongation to failure. The caption reports that this strength enhancement arises from rising dislocation density and grain refinement, whereas the reduced work-hardening capacity reflects the increased dislocation density. These curves support the core conclusion that warm MAF profoundly strengthens the steel via dislocation and grain-boundary strengthening, necessitating a modified Johnson–Cook model to capture the evolving plastic flow behavior.

Figure 11 presents a flow chart illustrating the algorithmic implementation of the VUMAT user material subroutine developed for ABAQUS to simulate warm multiaxial forging (MAF) of Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel using a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model. The chart traces the subroutine execution from initialization and material constant computation through each increment step. At the beginning of a step, the algorithm checks whether the step time equals zero: if yes, it calculates trial stress assuming pure elastic behavior and terminates; if no, it proceeds to calculate the deviatoric trial stress difference (sigdif). The subsequent logic branches on whether sigdif exceeds zero—yielding updates the stress via the constitutive relation, while non-yielding sets equivalent plastic strain to zero—before both paths converge to update stress and state-dependent variables (SDVs), and finally update internal and dissipated inelastic energy. The caption states that the implemented subroutine employs six SDVs to incorporate the modified J-C equation, enabling finite element modeling of dislocation strengthening and grain refinement during the MAF process.

Figure 12 presents finite-element simulation contour maps for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel subjected to warm multiaxial forging (MAF), comparing results after Pass 1, Pass 3, and Pass 5. The four rows correspond to (a) equivalent strain map (SDV1), (b) dislocation density map (SDV3, units m⁻², scale ~10¹⁵), (c) grain size distribution map (SDV6, units μm), and (d) flow stress map (SDV4, units MPa). For each variable, two sectional planes are shown per pass. Visual inspection reveals that equivalent strain, dislocation density, and flow stress progressively concentrate toward the specimen center, developing intense red-hot zones by Pass 5, while the grain size map exhibits an inverse pattern with finer grains in the center and coarser grains toward the outer regions. These trends demonstrate that increasing strain across the five MAF passes promotes dislocation accumulation and grain refinement, which in turn explains the marked rise in flow stress. The simulation results support the interpretation that suppressed dislocation climb and cross-slip at lower temperatures reduce dislocation mobility and annihilation at grain boundaries, thereby increasing both equivalent strain and dislocation density during warm MAF.

This figure presents a line graph (Fig. 13) comparing experimental and finite-element (FE) simulated yield strength versus the number of multiaxial forging (MAF) passes for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel. The x-axis represents the number of passes (0–5) and the y-axis represents yield strength in MPa. The solid line with square markers corresponds to experimental data, while the dashed line with circular markers corresponds to FE simulation results based on a modified Johnson-Cook model. Both datasets demonstrate a monotonic increase in yield strength with increasing passes, rising from approximately 380 MPa at 0 passes to about 1520 MPa experimentally and 1450 MPa in simulation after 5 passes. The predicted values closely match the experimental results at 0 and 3 passes but under-predict at 1 and 5 passes. The figure validates that the strain-dependent model integrating dislocation density and grain size captures the overall strengthening trend, while the accompanying text attributes the minor deviation at higher passes to the omission of texture effects in the simulation.

Figure 14 presents finite element simulation results showing yield strength distribution maps (state variable SDV5, in MPa) for Fe-30Mn-9Al-0.8C low-density steel during warm multiaxial forging (MAF). The color contour scale ranges from approximately 699 MPa (dark blue) to 1358 MPa (red). Three pairs of cross-sectional views, each labeled with distinct X-Y-Z coordinate orientations, display the progressive evolution of yield strength across successive MAF passes. From left to right, the overall yield strength levels increase markedly: the leftmost pair shows predominantly green and blue regions indicating lower strength, the middle pair exhibits yellow and orange zones of intermediate strength, and the rightmost pair reveals extensive red and orange cores signifying the highest strength. In all views, the strength is maximum at the geometric center and decreases toward the surfaces. These maps correspond to simulations integrating a modified Johnson-Cook constitutive model with strain-dependent dislocation density and grain size parameters, visually confirming that cumulative plastic strain during MAF progressively raises yield strength through the combined effects of dislocation strengthening and grain refinement.
